 {"id": "franken_networkx-do1", "title": "Traversal Extras: bfs_beam_edges, bfs/dfs_labeled_edges, generic_bfs_edges", "description": "## Scope
Implement 4 advanced traversal functions:
- bfs_beam_edges(G, source, value, width=None) — BFS with beam search pruning. At each level, only expand the top-width nodes as ranked by value(node). Returns edges of the beam-search tree. Useful for exploring large graphs with a heuristic.
- bfs_labeled_edges(G, source, sort_neighbors=None) — yield (u, v, label) where label is one of 'tree' (tree edge in BFS tree), 'level' (edge to node at same level), 'forward' (edge to already-visited node at deeper level), 'reverse' (back edge). Uses BFS with level tracking.
- dfs_labeled_edges(G, source=None, depth_limit=None) — yield (u, v, label) where label is 'tree' (DFS tree edge), 'forward' (edge to descendant not via tree), 'back' (edge to ancestor), 'cross' (edge to node in different subtree), 'nontree' (any non-tree edge). Standard DFS classification.
- generic_bfs_edges(G, source, neighbors=None, depth_limit=None, sort_neighbors=None) — BFS with customizable neighbor function. neighbors(node) returns iterable of adjacent nodes, allowing filtered/weighted BFS.

## Implementation Strategy
- All are generators (yield edges as discovered)
- bfs_labeled: track discovery level per node, classify edges by level comparison
- dfs_labeled: track discovery/finish times, classify by back/forward/cross rules
- generic_bfs: same as bfs_edges but calls neighbors(node) instead of G.neighbors(node)
- bfs_beam: at each level, sort candidates by value(), keep top width

## Test Requirements
- Tree edges form a valid spanning tree (connected, n-1 edges)
- Back edges in DFS create cycles
- Forward edges go to deeper nodes, cross edges go to different subtrees
- bfs_beam with width=None equals regular BFS
- generic_bfs with default neighbors equals regular BFS
- NX cross-validation: same edge classifications on small directed graphs", "status": "closed", "priority": 3, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-22T17:34:37.942681144Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-22T22:38:06.367225188Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-22T22:38:06.366727547Z", "close_reason": "Implemented 4 traversal functions: bfs_beam_edges, bfs_labeled_edges, dfs_labeled_edges, generic_bfs_edges.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "labels": ["algorithms", "traversal"]}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-e21", "title": "Extract scale_free_graph parameter sum epsilon to named constant", "description": "## What

Replace inline 1e-6 in fnx-generators scale_free_graph alpha+beta+gamma validation
with the named constant PARAM_SUM_EPSILON (defined as 1.0e-6).

## Status: COMPLETED (March 2026)

Line ~1214 in fnx-generators/src/lib.rs:
  Before: if (sum - 1.0).abs() > 1e-6
  After:  if (sum - 1.0).abs() > PARAM_SUM_EPSILON

## Why 1e-6 (not tighter)

This validates user-supplied parameters (alpha + beta + gamma = 1.0). Users pass
f64 values like 0.41 + 0.54 + 0.05 which may not sum to exactly 1.0 due to
representation error. 1e-6 is loose enough to accept reasonable inputs but tight
enough to catch obviously wrong parameters (e.g., forgetting to include gamma).

NetworkX uses the same tolerance: abs(alpha + beta + gamma - 1.0) >= 1e-6.

## Files Changed

- crates/fnx-generators/src/lib.rs (lines 17, ~1214)", "status": "closed", "priority": 1, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-25T03:04:56.851146350Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-25T03:05:02.646456488Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-25T03:05:02.645818911Z", "close_reason": "Inline 1e-6 replaced with PARAM_SUM_EPSILON constant. Verified.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0}
 {"id": "franken_networkx-e3ri", "title": "Generator parity follow-up: exact public random_unlabeled_rooted_tree API and seed adapters", "description": "## Scope
This rooted-forest port added exact private rooted-tree helpers, but the public `random_unlabeled_rooted_tree` surface still does not match NetworkX semantics. Seed handling also only covers stdlib-style seeds for the new forest path.

## Goal
Bring the public rooted-tree API to parity and decide whether to mirror NetworkX's broader seed adapter behavior (notably `number_of_trees`, null-tree contract, and NumPy-backed random-state inputs).

## Files
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py
- tests/python/test_generator_delegations_parity.py
- potentially additional generator parity tests", "status": "closed", "priority": 3, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-04-06T14:52:19.369182970Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-06T16:00:17.075403732Z", "closed_at": "2026-04-06T16:00:17.075140474Z", "close_reason": "Completed", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-guk", "title": "I/O: sparse6/graph6 and weighted edgelist formats", "description": "I/O: sparse6, graph6, and Weighted Edgelist Formats

Compact binary graph encodings (graph6/sparse6) and weighted edge list text format.

## Functions to Implement

### graph6
- `from_graph6_bytes(bytes_in) -> Graph`: Parse graph6 byte string into undirected graph.
- `to_graph6_bytes(G, nodes=None, header=True) -> bytes`: Serialize undirected graph to graph6 bytes.
- `read_graph6(path) -> Graph or list[Graph]`: Read graph6 file (one graph per line).
- `write_graph6(G, path, nodes=None, header=True)`: Write graph to graph6 file.
- `write_graph6_file(G_list, path, header=True)`: Write multiple graphs to graph6 file.
- `parse_graph6(string) -> Graph`: Parse graph6 string (text, not bytes).

### sparse6
- `from_sparse6_bytes(bytes_in) -> Graph`: Parse sparse6 byte string into undirected graph.
- `to_sparse6_bytes(G, nodes=None, header=True) -> bytes`: Serialize undirected graph to sparse6 bytes.
- `read_sparse6(path) -> Graph or list[Graph]`: Read sparse6 file.
- `write_sparse6(G, path, nodes=None, header=True)`: Write graph to sparse6 file.
- `parse_sparse6(string) -> Graph`: Parse sparse6 string.

### weighted_edgelist
- `read_weighted_edgelist(path, comments='#', delimiter=None, create_using=None, nodetype=None, encoding='utf-8') -> Graph`: Read edgelist with weight as third column.
- `write_weighted_edgelist(G, path, comments='#', delimiter=' ', encoding='utf-8')`: Write edgelist with weight column.

## Algorithmic Approach

### graph6 Encoding
- Header: optional ">>graph6<<" prefix.
- N(n): encode node count. n≤62: single byte (n+63). 63≤n≤258047: bytes 126, then 3 bytes. Larger: bytes 126,126, then 6 bytes.
- Adjacency: upper triangle of adjacency matrix packed into 6-bit chunks, each stored as byte+63.
- Decoding: reverse the process, reconstruct upper triangle.

### sparse6 Encoding
- Header: optional ">>sparse6<<" prefix, then ':'.
- N(n) same as graph6.
- Edges encoded as (b,x) pairs where b is 1 bit and x is k=ceil(log2(n)) bits. Uses run-length-like difference encoding.
- More compact than graph6 for sparse graphs (m << n^2/2).

### weighted_edgelist
- Text format: "node1 node2 weight" per line, with optional comments and configurable delimiter.

## Performance Targets

- Implement graph6/sparse6 encoding/decoding in Rust for performance.
- graph6 encode/decode 100K node graph: < 1 second.
- sparse6 should be faster than graph6 for sparse graphs (m < n^2/4).
- weighted_edgelist: streaming I/O, handle files larger than memory.

## Edge Cases

- graph6/sparse6 only support undirected simple graphs → raise NetworkXNotImplemented for directed/multi.
- Maximum node count for graph6: 68719476735 (2^36 - 1).
- Empty graph (0 edges), complete graph (all edges).
- sparse6: graph with no edges, graph with self-loops (sparse6 supports them, graph6 does not).
- weighted_edgelist: missing weight column → default weight 1.0.
- weighted_edgelist: non-numeric weight values.
- Node labels in weighted_edgelist: string vs integer type coercion.
- Padding bits in graph6/sparse6 (last byte may have unused bits).
- Header detection: auto-detect format from ">>graph6<<" vs ">>sparse6<<" prefix.

## Test Requirements

- Round-trip tests: to_bytes(from_bytes(data)) == data for both formats (byte-level parity).
- NX cross-validation: encode/decode must produce identical bytes as NetworkX for same graphs.
- Known graph tests: encode/decode Petersen graph, K5, K3,3, empty graphs against known byte strings.
- Correctness: decode(encode(G)).edges() == G.edges() for random graphs.
- Format detection tests: correctly identify graph6 vs sparse6 from header.
- weighted_edgelist round-trip: write then read preserves node names, edges, and weights.
- Property tests: encoded size of graph6 ≈ n^2/12 + n bytes, sparse6 ≈ proportional to m.
- Error handling: invalid bytes, truncated data, directed graph input.

## Implementation Notes

- Rust implementation for graph6/sparse6 bit manipulation (performance-critical).
- Python layer for file I/O and API compatibility.
- weighted_edgelist can be pure Python (I/O bound).
- Depends on core graph types.
- Reference: graph6/sparse6 spec at https://users.cecs.anu.edu.au/~bdm/data/formats.txt", "status": "closed", "priority": 3, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-21T19:21:00.309576537Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-22T17:34:25.225324128Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-22T17:34:25.224676622Z", "close_reason": "Completed", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "labels": ["io", "tier-3"]}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-gzf", "title": "Audit remaining inline magic numbers in test code", "description": "## What

The March 2026 audit found ~30 occurrences of inline 1e-10 and 1e-12 in TEST code
(assert statements). While these don't affect production behavior, they should be
reviewed for consistency:

1. Test assertions using 1e-10 should consider using FLOW_EPSILON or a test-specific constant
2. Test assertions using 1e-12 should consider using DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON
3. Any test using a DIFFERENT epsilon than production code is suspicious

## Current State

rg output shows ~20 test assertions with inline 1e-12 (lines 22153-22433) and
~5 with inline 1e-10 (lines 26795-26854). These are in:
- Distance measure tests
- Link prediction score tests
- Shortest path correctness tests

## Risk Assessment

LOW — these are test tolerances, not production code. But inconsistency between
test tolerance and production tolerance could mask bugs where production uses a
tighter epsilon than the test checks.

## Recommended Approach

Define test-specific constants:
```rust
#[cfg(test)]
const TEST_FLOAT_TOLERANCE: f64 = 1.0e-12;
```

Then replace all test `1e-12` with `TEST_FLOAT_TOLERANCE`.

## Files To Change

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (test module only, ~25 occurrences)", "status": "closed", "priority": 3, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-25T03:06:23.081392110Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-25T03:22:01.787458269Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-25T03:22:01.786811665Z", "close_reason": "Added TEST_TOLERANCE constant. Replaced ~115 inline 1e-12/1e-10 in test assertions with TEST_TOLERANCE. All 595 Rust tests pass.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "dependencies": [{"issue_id": "franken_networkx-gzf", "depends_on_id": "franken_networkx-hl4", "type": "blocks", "created_at": "2026-03-25T03:06:30.667862554Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "metadata": "{}", "thread_id": ""}]}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-h0so", "title": "NX-REPLACE: Isomorphism — is_isomorphic, vf2pp variants, graph_edit_distance", "description": "## Functions (7) — isomorphism
1. is_isomorphic (__init__.py:6829) — Rust impl exists but Python overrides for callback support
2. vf2pp_is_isomorphic (__init__.py:6846) — VF2++ exists in Rust
3. vf2pp_isomorphism (__init__.py:6859) — returns mapping
4. vf2pp_all_isomorphisms (__init__.py:6867) — yields all mappings
5. graph_edit_distance (__init__.py:7213) — NP-hard
6. optimal_edit_paths (__init__.py:7029) — edit path enumeration
7. optimize_edit_paths (__init__.py:7049) — iterative GED optimization

## Implementation plan
Phase 1 (high value): Wire existing Rust is_isomorphic/vf2pp for common case (no callbacks). Detect when node_match/edge_match are None and use fast Rust path.
Phase 2 (fallback): When callbacks provided, delegate to NX (justified — callbacks are Python objects).
Phase 3 (optional): graph_edit_distance is NP-hard, rarely used — acceptable NX delegation.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_isomorphism_parity.py:
- test_is_isomorphic_same_graph: G ≅ G is True
- test_is_isomorphic_relabeled: G ≅ relabel(G) is True
- test_is_isomorphic_different: C4 ≇ P4
- test_is_isomorphic_with_node_match: verify callback respected
- test_is_isomorphic_uses_rust_when_no_callbacks: verify fast path
- test_vf2pp_is_isomorphic_matches_nx: compare results
- test_vf2pp_isomorphism_mapping_valid: verify mapping preserves edges
- test_vf2pp_all_isomorphisms_count: compare count with NX
- test_graph_edit_distance_matches_nx: compare on small graphs (n<=6)

Run: pytest tests/python/test_isomorphism_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: conditional dispatch (Rust fast / NX fallback)
- tests/python/test_isomorphism_parity.py: new test file", "status": "closed", "priority": 3, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-04-03T00:19:46.680320244Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-05T23:42:17.415306436Z", "closed_at": "2026-04-05T23:42:17.415016898Z", "close_reason": "Added Rust fast path for vf2pp_is_isomorphic when node_label=None (redirects to existing Rust is_isomorphic). is_isomorphic already had Rust fast path for no-callback case. Remaining NX delegations: vf2pp_isomorphism (mapping), vf2pp_all_isomorphisms (enumeration), graph_edit_distance/optimal_edit_paths/optimize_edit_paths (NP-hard, justified NX delegation). All 1378 tests pass.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "dependencies": [{"issue_id": "franken_networkx-h0so", "depends_on_id": "franken_networkx-okpa", "type": "blocks", "created_at": "2026-04-03T00:21:20.842291879Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "metadata": "{}", "thread_id": ""}]}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-qwd5", "title": "NX-REPLACE Graph operators: line_graph, inverse_line_graph, power, stochastic, disjoint_union (6 fns)", "description": "## Functions (6) — need new Rust implementations
1. line_graph (__init__.py:1458) — L(G): edges become nodes, shared endpoints become edges. O(E*d_max).
2. inverse_line_graph (__init__.py:3806) — Whitney's theorem: recover G from L(G). Non-trivial.
3. make_max_clique_graph (__init__.py:1473) — clique graph: cliques become nodes, shared nodes become edges. Uses find_cliques (already in Rust).
4. power (__init__.py:1487) — G^k: add edge (u,v) if dist(u,v) <= k. Uses BFS (already in Rust).
5. stochastic_graph (__init__.py:1367) — normalize out-edge weights to sum to 1 per node.
6. disjoint_union (__init__.py:1496) + disjoint_union_all (__init__.py:2311) — union with node relabeling to avoid collisions.

## Implementation plan
- line_graph: for each pair of edges sharing a node, add edge in L(G). Rust impl is straightforward.
- inverse_line_graph: more complex (Whitney's algorithm). Could remain NX for V1 if too complex.
- make_max_clique_graph: enumerate cliques (existing), then connect overlapping cliques.
- power: BFS from each node up to depth k, add edges. Reuses existing BFS.
- stochastic_graph: iterate nodes, normalize out-edge weights. Simple Rust.
- disjoint_union: relabel nodes with prefix/offset, then merge.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_graph_operators_parity.py:
- test_line_graph_triangle: L(K3) == K3
- test_line_graph_path: L(P4) has correct structure
- test_line_graph_matches_nx: general parity on 5+ graphs
- test_power_graph_matches_nx: G^2, G^3 on path/cycle graphs
- test_stochastic_graph_row_sums: verify each row sums to 1.0
- test_stochastic_graph_matches_nx: compare edge weights
- test_disjoint_union_no_overlap: verify no shared nodes
- test_disjoint_union_matches_nx: compare node/edge counts
- test_make_max_clique_graph_matches_nx: compare on small graphs

## Depends on
- okpa (graph utilities) — need stable node key handling for product graphs
- 06yo (attribute access) — stochastic_graph needs edge attribute setting

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: implement 5 algorithms
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: add 5 bindings
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewire 6 functions
- tests/python/test_graph_operators_parity.py: new test file", "status": "closed", "priority": 2, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-04-03T02:43:42.696813163Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-03T18:52:00.365479145Z", "closed_at": "2026-04-03T18:52:00.365210857Z", "close_reason": "Completed native parity rewires for line_graph, make_max_clique_graph, power, stochastic_graph, disjoint_union, and disjoint_union_all; left inverse_line_graph for follow-up franken_networkx-zgfd", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-rbda", "title": "DOCS: First-time user experience — quickstart, examples, API reference", "description": "## Gap
README.md has a Quick Start section but the user experience for someone discovering the library is thin:
- No expanded API reference (just the algorithm table in README)
- No migration guide from NetworkX
- No performance comparison data visible to users
- No contributing guide
- No doctest verification that examples actually work

Note: PyPI publication (ciq6) and CI (zrlb) are now COMPLETE. Release workflow and E2E tests exist. This bead focuses on user-facing documentation.

## What to create

### 1. docs/ directory structure
- docs/quickstart.md: expanded tutorial with real-world graph analysis examples
  - Social network analysis (build graph, find communities, compute centrality)
  - Road network shortest paths (weighted Dijkstra)
  - Biological network (bipartite, clustering)
- docs/migration.md: 'switching from NetworkX' guide
  - Side-by-side code comparisons for top 20 patterns
  - What's identical, what differs, known limitations
  - Backend mode (zero changes) vs standalone mode
- docs/algorithms.md: algorithm reference with complexity and examples
  - Grouped by family (shortest path, centrality, community, etc.)
  - Each entry: signature, complexity, example, NX equivalent
- docs/performance.md: benchmark comparison vs NetworkX
  - Pagerank: 20x speedup chart
  - Shortest path: comparison data
  - Community detection: comparison data
  - Memory usage comparison
- docs/backend.md: NetworkX backend integration guide
  - Setup, configuration, which algorithms dispatch to Rust
  - Which algorithms still fall back to NX and why
- docs/contributing.md: developer guide
  - How to build (maturin develop)
  - How to run tests (pytest, cargo test)
  - Code structure (11 crates, Python wrapper)
  - How to add a new algorithm

### 2. Example scripts
- examples/basic_usage.py: standalone quickstart
- examples/backend_mode.py: using as NX backend
- examples/social_network.py: real-world analysis
- examples/benchmark_comparison.py: reproduce perf claims

### 3. PyPI metadata enhancement
- pyproject.toml: update long_description, classifiers, URLs
  - project-urls: Documentation (link to docs/), Repository (GitHub), Changelog
  - classifiers for Topic::Scientific, Status::Production, Framework::NetworkX

### 4. Doctest verification
- Create scripts/verify_docs.py that extracts code blocks from .md files and runs them
- Add to CI as a documentation gate

## Testing requirements
- Every code example in docs/ must be runnable and produce claimed output
- scripts/verify_docs.py: extract and run all Python code blocks from docs/*.md
- Verify all links in README/docs are valid (no dead links)
- Verify pyproject.toml metadata renders correctly on PyPI

## Files to create
- docs/quickstart.md, migration.md, algorithms.md, performance.md, backend.md, contributing.md
- examples/basic_usage.py, backend_mode.py, social_network.py, benchmark_comparison.py
- scripts/verify_docs.py: documentation verifier
- Update pyproject.toml metadata

## Acceptance criteria
- New user can follow quickstart to working code in < 5 minutes
- Migration guide covers top 20 NetworkX patterns with before/after
- All code examples verified runnable via scripts/verify_docs.py
- PyPI page shows rich metadata with description, links, classifiers", "status": "closed", "priority": 3, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-04-06T15:50:34.163105605Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-04-07T02:03:28.510857373Z", "closed_at": "2026-04-07T02:03:28.510596129Z", "close_reason": "Added first-time-user docs set, runnable examples, docs verifier, CI docs gate, and richer PyPI metadata", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0}

 {"id": "franken_networkx-y1g", "title": "Coloring & Planarity: equitable coloring, chromatic polynomial, embeddings", "description": "## Scope
Implement 4 coloring and planarity functions:
- `equitable_color` — find equitable k-coloring (color classes differ by at most 1 in size)
- `chromatic_polynomial` — compute the chromatic polynomial of a graph
- `combinatorial_embedding_to_pos` — convert combinatorial planar embedding to coordinate positions
- `apply_matplotlib_colors` — utility to apply color scheme to matplotlib visualization

## Algorithmic Approach
- equitable_color: Use the Kierstead-Kostochka procedure. Start with a greedy coloring, then equalize class sizes by recoloring chains. O(V * k) where k is number of colors.
- chromatic_polynomial: Deletion-contraction recursion. P(G,k) = P(G-e,k) - P(G/e,k). NP-hard in general. Use memoization on graph isomorphism classes. For small graphs (<20 nodes) this is feasible. Can also use Whitney rank polynomial relationship.
- combinatorial_embedding_to_pos: Given face structure from planar embedding, use Tutte's barycentric method or Schnyder realizer to assign coordinates. O(V) for Tutte embedding.
- apply_matplotlib_colors: Simple utility, map color indices to matplotlib colormap values.

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/coloring/` and `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/planarity.py`
- chromatic_polynomial could benefit from Rust for memoized recursion

## Edge Cases
- equitable_color: graph requiring more than Delta+1 colors (should raise)
- chromatic_polynomial: very slow for dense graphs > 15 nodes
- combinatorial_embedding_to_pos: non-planar input (should raise or handle gracefully)

## Test Requirements
- equitable_color: verify valid coloring AND equitability (class sizes differ by <= 1)
- chromatic_polynomial: verify P(G,k) = 0 for k < chi(G), P(K_n, k) = k(k-1)...(k-n+1)
- Cross-validate against NetworkX", "status": "closed", "priority": 3, "issue_type": "task", "created_at": "2026-03-21T19:14:02.971518597Z", "created_by": "ubuntu", "updated_at": "2026-03-23T07:35:00.719565439Z", "closed_at": "2026-03-23T07:35:00.719053861Z", "close_reason": "Implemented 3 coloring/planarity: equitable_color, chromatic_polynomial, combinatorial_embedding_to_pos.", "source_repo": ".", "compaction_level": 0, "original_size": 0, "labels": ["algorithms", "coloring", "planarity", "tier-3"]}
